full_response:
Solution Outline
We are asked only for a “naïve’’ (i.e. simplest-possible) algorithm.  
The straightforward idea is to inspect every unordered pair of launch-sites and measure their Euclidean distance.

Notation
n = |L|  (number of launch-sites)  
L = {l1 , … , ln}, li = (xi , yi)

Algorithm (pseudocode)

```
Naive-Closest-Pair(L)
1   n ← |L|
2   for i ← 1 to n–1               ▹ examine every unordered pair
3         for j ← i+1 to n
4               ▹ Euclidean distance between li and lj
5               d ← √( (xi – xj)^2 + (yi – yj)^2 )
6               if d < 1
7                     return (li , lj)     ▹ viability violated
8   return FALSE                           ▹ all pairs ≥ 1 apart
```

Correctness Proof  
We prove that the algorithm returns a violating pair iff one exists.

• If the algorithm returns (li , lj):  
  by line 6 the distance computed is < 1, hence the pair is indeed a violating pair.

• Conversely, suppose a violating pair (lp , lq) exists.  
  Because the two nested loops enumerate every unordered pair once, the iteration (i = p, j = q) (or the symmetric one) will be executed. The distance computed at that iteration is < 1, line 6 becomes true and the algorithm returns that pair (possibly earlier if another violating pair exists). Therefore, if a violating pair exists, the procedure will return a pair and never reach line 8.

Thus the algorithm is correct. ∎

Running-Time Analysis  
There are (n choose 2) = n(n–1)/2 ≈ Θ(n²) iterations of the innermost body.  
Each iteration performs O(1) arithmetic (two subtractions, two squares, one addition, one square-root, one comparison).  
Hence total running time

T(n) = Θ(n²) · O(1) = Θ(n²).

Space Usage  
Apart from a few scalar variables, no additional storage is used: O(1) extra space.

Therefore the naïve algorithm runs in Θ(n²) time and O(1) space.

\boxed{\text{Naïve algorithm: check every pair; time } \Theta(n^{2}),\; \text{space } O(1).}  

Confidence: 0.34
extracted_answer: \text{Naïve algorithm: check every pair; time } \Theta(n^{2}),\; \text{space } O(1).
